does: This paper systematizes preference alignment techniques for generative music AI, addressing the disconnect between standard loss functions and human musical taste. It surveys recent methods including MusicRL's large-scale preference learning, diffusion-based preference optimization approaches like DiffRhythm+, and inference-time techniques such as Text2midi-InferAlign. The work identifies music-specific alignment challenges including temporal coherence maintenance, harmonic consistency preservation, and reliable subjective quality assessment across diverse listener preferences. By synthesizing these approaches, the paper demonstrates how preference learning can improve alignment between generated music and human evaluation criteria, moving beyond generic fidelity metrics toward nuanced artistic quality.
connects: This work builds on the recent wave of preference-based alignment methods in generative AI, extending techniques from vision and language models into the music domain. It engages with reinforcement learning from human feedback frameworks, diffusion model optimization strategies, and inference-time adaptation approaches developed across multiple AI modalities. The paper responds to limitations of earlier music generation systems that optimized for acoustic fidelity while ignoring compositional structure and listener satisfaction. It draws on prior work establishing that human preferences in creative domains are multifaceted and context-dependent, while building toward systematic musicological grounding in preference models.
enables: This research opens pathways for interactive music composition tools that adapt to individual or collaborative preferences in real-time, and for personalized music generation systems that respect diverse cultural and stylistic preferences. It establishes foundations for long-form music generation with coherent structure, addressing current scalability limits in preference modeling. The framework enables future work in cross-cultural preference alignment, multi-stakeholder composition scenarios, and music systems that balance artistic innovation with user satisfaction. It also points toward music-specific evaluation benchmarks and preference datasets, and suggests how preference alignment might extend to other structured creative domains requiring temporal consistency and domain expertise.
